title: Lesson 339
transcript: Locks. Locks are obviously a very important control in physical security, but one really important thing to remember about locks is that there has never yet been a lock built that is unpickable. As a result, locks are a perfect example of a delay control. A lock is only gonna delay an attacker from getting in. Never going to prevent an attacker. So locks are considered a delay control. If you want a great example, let's go on YouTube and look up the lock picking lawyer. Hilarious channel. He can break into pretty much any lock in like three seconds with a blade of grass and an eyelash. It's just amazing. So locks are considered delay devices. Now you do, for the exam, need to know about a number of different types of locks. At a high level, there's kind of two major categories of locks. Mechanical locks and electronic locks. It's just like it sounds, like mechanical locks rely on some sort of mechanical mechanism and electronic locks rely on computer systems. So let's go through each of these and define them. So a mechanical keyed lock is what we're all very familiar with, how we probably get into most of our homes and things like that. A mechanical keyed lock is where you have a key that you use to unlock a door. The big downside of keyed mechanical locks is key management, trying to keep track of all the keys. If someone loses a key, I don't know where it is, you might have to pay to rekey all your locks. Or if someone leaves the organization under not great circumstances, under duress, maybe, and they take the keys with them, you might have to pay to rekey all your locks. And keeping track of all these keys is a really challenging thing to do. So that's kind of one of the major issues with mechanical key locks. Mechanical combination locks, a great example of that is what you can see here, right? We probably all had these in high school at our gym locker, right? You turn the lock a little bit this way and then back to this number and then forward to that number. That's a mechanical combination lock. A magnetic mechanical lock is, a great example of this is when you go into, say, a clothing store and they have those security tags on the clothing. And you may have wondered, right, if you're like trying to pull it apart, it won't pop open. And yet when you go up to the tilt of pay, the checkout person just kind of waves the tag over a little spot on their checkout counter and it just pops open. How does that work? There's a really strong magnet built into the checkout counter. And it's a magnetic lock. So you put the lock over that strong magnet, it causes the lock to pop open. So that's an example of a magnetic lock. Now kind of the major downside with all these mechanical locks is they do not have any built-in logging and monitoring capability. A mechanical keyed combination magnetic lock, they don't tell you when they've been unlocked or relocked or anything like that. So if you wanna have a monitoring capability, you have to provide it entirely separately with a camera system or something like that. And that leads to one of the big benefits of electronic locks. It's quite easy to build in a logging and monitoring capability. With proximity RFID cards, electronic combination locks, and especially biometric systems, it's very easy to keep a log of when the door was unlocked, with what combination, or with what proximity card, or with biometric systems, specifically who unlocked. the door. So let's go through each of these. So a proximity RFID, this is where, I mean there's even older school, older style magnetic swipe cards, but this is where someone has to have some sort of card in their possession. And if it's wireless, they just have to put the card near the reader. And the wireless technology that's usually used here is RFID, radio frequency identification. So if you have like an employee ID card that you just have to hold near the door and the door goes beep and opens up, That's a wireless proximity card or RFID card. Now the older school way of doing this, you've probably experienced this in hotels and stuff, right? You have to, they give you a card, they have to swipe through a reader. That's a magnetic reader. It's still an electronic lock. An electronic combination lock is where you have to type in a pin code, but there's an electronic system behind the scenes. And so one of the cool things you can do here is that you can give every employee, say, a different combination. And so to an extent, you might know who's typing in the code because each employee might only have their own combination. but you don't entirely know, right? And that's the big downside with combination and card access systems, proximity cards. You don't specifically know who actually has the card in their possession or who's typing in the combination. Maybe someone just found a card on the ground and it said, you know, this belongs to this organization. So they walked up to the building and used it and got in. You know, whoever, you don't specifically know who's holding the card or who's typing in the combination. And that's where we get to biometric locks. The huge advantage of biometric locks over every other one listed here is that a biometric lock can uniquely identify a specific individual. And we'll talk about different types of biometric systems in domain five. Finger paint, facial recognition, iris, retinal, hand geometry. There's all sorts of different types of biometric systems that can be used to uniquely identify an individual. which is obviously very beneficial. Now here's a good exam question that you might come across. What determines the security of a combination lock? This is a really interesting question to kind of think about for a second. There's obviously a number of factors that determine the security of a lock. Is it the type of metal the lock is made out of? Or is it how many tumblers it has inside the lock? Or is it the number of different combinations that might be possible? What is overall the biggest determining factor for how secure a combination lock is? What do you think? The answer is it's the complexity of the combination. That is the most important defining factor of the security. What defines the security of a combination lock is the complexity of the combination. And final little piece we'll cover here, which we're actually gonna talk about in a lot more detail in Domain 5, is smart cards and biometric systems. These are two commonly used methods that are used to unlock doors and gain physical access and physical security. And we'll talk about them, like I said, in more detail in Domain 5.
